coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots supported ZnO sphere based photocatalyst for dye degradation application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Velumani, Arun
Sengodan, Prabhu
Arumugam, Priyadharsan
Rajendran, Ramesh
Santhanam, Sivakumar
Palanisamy, Maadeswaran
coredata.dc:description: A Carbon quantum dots supported ZnO hollow Sphere (ZnO/C-dots) were synthesized through a solvothermal method using polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) as a solvent. The phase and crystal structure of as-prepared ZnO/C-dots photocatalyst were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The surface morphology and size of the composite were analyzed using field emission scanning microscopy (FE-SEM). The optical properties of the as-prepared nanocomposites were examined using UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer. The photocatalytic activity of pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV–Visible light irradiation. The ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites exhibited maximum photocatalytic MB dye degradation efficiency of 96% which is much higher that the pure ZnO (63%). The enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnO/C-dots is due to the extended light absorption in the visible region and suppressed photoexcited electron-hole pair recombination rate. Moreover, the activity of photocatalyst after five cycles exhibits high stability, which is vital for the sustainable photocatalytic procedures. It is concluded that the prepared ZnO/C-dots composite have low cost, good stability and has a great potential application for Photocatalytic dye degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO hollow Sphere
Carbon quantum dots
Optical property
Photocatalytic activity
Photostability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reusability and stability of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–visible absorption spectrum and (b) Excited wavelength dependent emission spectra of C-dots. Inset figure show yellow emission of as prepared C-dots under illumination of C-dots.
Powder XRD patterns of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites.

FE-SEM image of (a–b) ZnO hollow sphere, (c–d) ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites, (e) TEM and (f) HR-TEM images of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites (C-dots on the surface of the ZnO sphere were marked by red circle).
FT-IR Spectra of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites.
(a) UV–visible spectra of pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots and (b) Tauc plot of pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots derived from UV–visible spectra.
UV–visible absorption spectra for degradation of MB on (a) ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites under UV–Visible light and solar light irradiation.
Degradation efficiency of MB as a function of irradiation time with UV–Visible light and solar light in the presence of different of ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites.
PL Spectra of ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites (Excitation wavelength: 290 nm).
(a) Transient photocurrent (i–t), (b) Nyquist plots of ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites.
Schematic diagram for photocatalytic MB dye degradation of ZnO/C-dots composite under UV–Visible light irradiation.
Effect of the pH for the ZnO/c-dots nanocomposites.
Comparison of C-dots based nanocomposites assisted photolytic degradation (%) of previously reported.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots supported ZnO sphere based photocatalyst for dye degradation application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Carbon quantum dots supported ZnO hollow Sphere (ZnO/C-dots) were synthesized through a solvothermal method using polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) as a solvent. The phase and crystal structure of as-prepared ZnO/C-dots photocatalyst were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The surface morphology and size of the composite were analyzed using field emission scanning microscopy (FE-SEM). The optical properties of the as-prepared nanocomposites were examined using UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer. The photocatalytic activity of pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV–Visible light irradiation. The ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites exhibited maximum photocatalytic MB dye degradation efficiency of 96% which is much higher that the pure ZnO (63%). The enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnO/C-dots is due to the extended light absorption in the visible region and suppressed photoexcited electron-hole pair recombination rate. Moreover, the activity of photocatalyst after five cycles exhibits high stability, which is vital for the sustainable photocatalytic procedures. It is concluded that the prepared ZnO/C-dots composite have low cost, good stability and has a great potential application for Photocatalytic dye degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface and ground water are polluting by mixing of toxic heavy metals and dissolved dyes from various industries and it causes the several environmental issues which creates the concern among the growing population [1–3]. The treatment of wastewater containing the synthetic organic dye is of paramount importance and urgency. However, due to its chemical stability and complex structure, it is almost impossible to spontaneously degrade under the natural condition [4,5]. The advanced techniques such as biological, electro-oxidation, adsorption and photocatalysis techniques have been exploited to eliminate the toxic chemicals from wastewater [6–10]. Among the available techniques, photocatalysis is the efficient, sustainable and economically viable technique. In the photocatalysis process, semiconductor is using as catalyst to promote the degradation of toxic materials in wastewater. It is being reported that the various metal oxides semiconductor, such as TiO2, WO3, NiO, CuO, ZnO, CeO2, MnO2, α-Fe2O3 etc., used as photocatalyst in photocatlysis dye degradation process due to their photo and chemical stabilities [11–18]. Among the metal oxides semiconductor, Zinc Oxide (ZnO), is an important n-type semiconducting materials has been extensively used in wastewater treatment because of its low toxicity, earth abundance and high electron mobility [19,20]. However, there are hurdles to attain the high photocatalytic efficiency due to i) wide band gap (3.37 eV) could limit the visible light absorption and ii) predominant photoexcited charge recombination rate [21]. The visible light absorption could be extended by doping with metal ions or introduction of light sensitizers like quantum dots and carbon dots on the surface of ZnO and also which could suppress the charge recombination rate [22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, attracted more research interests due to their favorable characteristics of excellent water solubility, better biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent optical and chemical stabilities [23]. The carbon exhibits photo-induced electron transfer properties, which could be modified the intrinsic properties of semiconductors that could be exploited for various applications. Therefore, construction of composite with metal oxide semiconductors could be modified the intrinsic properties of semiconductors that could be exploited for various applications. For example, Xin et al. prepared carbon quantum dots/ZnO nanoflowers composites via simple chemical method and investigated their photocatalytic performance by degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. The results revealed that the nanocomposites exhibits the maximum efficiency of 98% which is much higher than that of pristine ZnO [24]. Bozetine et al. reported the synthesis of ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CQDs) nanocomposites by hydrothermal method and studied their photocatalytic activity by degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under simulated solar light. The degradation efficiency was 94% degradation; it is higher than compared with pure ZnO [25]. Therefore, it is explored that the photocatalytic activity of ZnO is increased by formation of nanocomposite with CODs. Herein we prepared ZnO/C-Dots nano architecture by chemical method. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared photocatalyst was evaluated through degradation of waste water containing MB dye molecule under solar light irradiation. The morphology, structural and optical properties of the prepared catalyst was studied and also possible photocatalytic mechanism was discussed with some experimental evidence.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O-99.5%), glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆-99%), urea (CO(NH2)2–99.5%), copra oil, polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400-98%), methylene blue (MB) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Merck reagent company in India. All the chemical reagents were analytically pure and without further purification.

In a typical process, a mixed powder of 10 g of glucose and 2 g of urea was added into heated copra oil (100 mL, about 250 °C) and the color of the mixture turned dark brown from white under the mechanical stirring for 3 min, which indicated the formation of C-dots. After cooling to room temperature, 30 mL of chloroform was added into the reaction system and the synthesized C-dots would be transferred to water phase due to their excellent hydrophilic properties. The upper edible oil was removed by separation funnel and reused for further preparation. The obtained C-dots aqueous solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

In the typical of synthesis process, 0.2 M of Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O) and 0.4 M of urea (CO(NH2)2) was dissolved in the 60 mL of polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) to form a clear solution under 90 min vigorous magnetic stirring. The prepared solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in hot air oven at 180 °C for 12 h and then autoclave was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. The white precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol and DI water several times. Finally, the collected sample was dried in a hot air oven at 80 °C overnight.

ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were synthesized by the solvothermal method. Typically, 0.2 M of Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O) and 0.4 M of urea (CO(NH2)2) were dissolved in the 60 mL of polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) solution with l mL of previously prepared CQDs solution. The solution was magnetic stirring vigorously for 90 min at room temperature. The final solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 15 h. The obtained product was centrifugation and washed with deionized water and ethanol to remove the impurities. Finally, the product was dried in a hot air oven for 12 h at 80 °C.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) using Rigaku D/Max Ultima III X-ray diffractometer with a CuKα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm) operated at 40 kV. The surface morphology and particle size of the obtained samples was investigated using a JSM-7500 F field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) with an accelerating voltage 0.1 kV–30 kV. The internal morphologies of the nanocomposites were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-600). The optical absorption of the prepared samples were characterized by a UV-2600 ultraviolet/visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were analyzed using JASCO FP-6500 with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded by Bruker a NEXUS-470 in the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 using KBr pellet technique.

Photocurrent measurements were performed in a three-electrode cell configuration that was connected to a SP-150 electrochemical workstation (BioLogic Science Instruments, France). In the three electrode system, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl and AgCl solution) was used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. 2 mg of as-prepared samples were dispersed 0.5 mL ethanol and 10 μL Nafion solution with help of ultrasonication for 2 h to obtain a homogeneous ink solution. The solution was spin coated (KW-4A-High precision spin coater) on Fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass substrate with rotation speed 1000 rpm for 10 min. The spin coated FTO substrate were naturally dried under atmosphere condition. 0.5 M Na2SO4 was act as an electrolyte solution. A 150 W xenon lamp was used as light source to simulate solar light. The light intensity is 100 mW/cm2, which was measured using solar power meter (TES 1333). The photocurrent versus time (i-t curve) recorded by every 50 s light on and off condition. The EIS Nyquist plots were carryout by AC voltage (10 mV) and frequency range of 1 MHz–0.1 Hz under open circuit potential.

The photocatalytic activities of the prepared ZnO hollow sphere and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were evaluated through the degradation of methylene blue (MB). The photocatalytic experiment was carried out in 100 mL aqueous solution with 10 mg/L MB. In the photocatalytic process, a 100 mg/L photocatalyst is dispersed in aqueous dye solution and kept for adsorption-desorption equilibrium for 30 min in the dark condition. The container with photocatalyst and aqueous dye solution was irradiated by 500 W halogen lamp and solar light to initiate the photocatalytic process. Every 5 min time interval, 4 mL of dye solutions were taken from the reactor and separated from the catalyst particles by centrifugation at 5500 rpm for 15 min. The separated MB dye solution was measured on JASCO V-670 double beam spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic dye degradation efficiency were calculated the following Eq. (1).
(1)
η =
C o
− C
C o
X 100 %
where, C
o is the initial concentration of dye at the time t0 and C is the concentration of dye at the time t [26].

The optical properties of as prepared C-dots were studied by using UV–Visible absorption and photoluminescence spectroscope techniques (Fig. 1 (a)&b). In the UV–Visible spectrum, the absorption band between 220 and 270 nm is due to the π–π* electron transition of CC bonds and absorption band between 370 and 400 nm corresponds to the n–π* electron transition of C–O bonds (Fig. 1(a)). The PL spectra were recorded for different excitation wavelengths ranging from 345 to 490 nm shown in Fig. 1(b). The emission intensity was decreased as the excitation wavelength increased from 345 to 490 nm. This emission was mainly attributed to defect states and intrinsic defects of the C-dots.

The crystalline structure and phase purity of the as prepared ZnO hollow spheres, and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and shown in Fig. 2 . Both pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots composite consistent with the hexagonal wurtzite structure (JPCDS 36–1451) with diffraction peaks at 31.8°, 34.4°, 36.3°, 47.5°, 56.6°, 62.9° and 68.0° correspond to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) crystal planes, respectively [27]. The diffraction peaks of C-dots species are not observed in the ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite, due to their amorphous nature. No diffraction peaks from other impurities were observed in the XRD patterns, which imply the high purity of samples.

The morphologies and microstructures of the ZnO and ZnO/C-dots composites were examined by FE-SEM techniques. Fig. 3 (a) and b shows the FE-SEM image of pure ZnO sample and clearly it appears well-defined hollow spherical architectures with an average diameter of 800 nm, where the nanoflakes were regularly arranged to form the hollow sphere. Whereas, the closed sphere with diameter around 800 nm was obtained for ZnO/C-dots composites (Fig. 3c and d).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images of prepared ZnO/C-dots composite are shown in Fig. 3 e&f. The synthesized composite were in spherical shape with the diameter of 500 nm Fig. 3(e). The flake like nanosheets was aggregated to form the sphere. As shown in Fig. 3(f) , small size (2–5 nm) C-dots were attached on the surface of ZnO sphere to form the ZnO/C-dots heterostructure.

The chemical structure and vibration modes of the prepared samples were analyzed by the FT-IR spectroscopy technique. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the prepared ZnO hollow sphere and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 4 (a–b). The broad absorption band at 3408 cm−1 which is corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations. The bands at 1615 and 1420 cm−1 related to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of the O–C–O. The peaks at about 1120 and 904 cm−1 were assigned to the C–O bonds and epoxy groups (C-dots) [28]. The strong absorption bands at about 463 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O [29].

The optical absorption property of the prepared samples was measured by UV–Vis spectroscopy technique. Pure ZnO hollow sphere exhibited the absorption edge at 400 nm so it could majorly active in the UV region of the solar spectrum [30]. In the present work, C-dots were incorporated with the ZnO hollow sphere to enhance the light absorption property of ZnO. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the optical absorption was extended in the visible region for ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites as compared with pure ZnO hollow sphere. The optical band gap of the as-prepared samples was determined by Tauc plot method [31]. Fig. 5(b) shows the calculated optical band gap values of 3.14 eV and 2.86 eV for ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites, respectively. The band gap value of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites is blue shifted compared with that of pure ZnO hollow sphere, is due to incorporation of visible light absorbing (nm) C-dots. This result can be effectively exploded for photocatalytic MB dye degradation.

To investigate the photocatalytic activity of the prepared materials, MB was used as a model organic pollutant and photocatalytic activity of material was evaluated by degradation of MB dye solution under UV–Visible light and solar light irradiation. Fig. 6 (a–b) shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of MB solution in the presence of ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites under different light illumination time. Fig. 7 (a) shows the calculated degradation efficiency of ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite are 63% and 96% of MB dye respectively, after 30 min of UV–Visible light irradiation. The ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites shows the best photocatalytic activity when compared with that of pure ZnO.

At the initial stage there is no degradation was observed, upon irradiating the UV–visible light on the photocatalytic reaction, the degradation rate of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite was reached 70% within 20 min, whereas the degradation rate of pure ZnO is only 23%. The degradation rate was reached 96% for ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite when the photocatalytic reaction time reaches 30 min. Meanwhile only 63% photocatalytic degradation efficiency was obtained for pure ZnO. Fig. 6(c–d) shows the photocatalytic degradation of catalyst and MB remains constant after illumination with sunlight irradiation for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 7(b) the calculated degradation efficiency of MB was accelerated under solar irradiation for 30 min, reached 61%, and 97% corresponds to ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites, respectively. The ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites exhibits the 96% and 97% of photocatalytic MB degradation efficiency under UV–Visible and solar light irradiation, respectively. The better photocatalytic MB dye degradation efficiency is mainly due to the formation ZnO/C-dots heterostructure (97%) under solar light irradiation [32]. The photocatalytic activity of as prepared ZnO/C-dots was compared to the previously reported activity of various photocatalysts as shown in Table 1 . From Table 1, it is clear that the ZnO/C-dots materials showed enhanced visible-light activity compared to the previously reported other catalysts.

The emission and charge transfer properties of prepared materials were examined by PL technique. Fig. 8 shows PL spectra of the pure ZnO hollow sphere and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 8, the PL spectra of both pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite exhibit two bands at 384 nm in the blue region and another weak and broad green-peak at 553 nm. The blue emission is mainly originated from the defect center, while the green emission attributed to the near band-edge emission (NBE) from the recombination of photo excited electron and holes [44]. It is notably observed that the quenching in intensity of PL emission of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite when compared with pure ZnO hollow sphere. This result is mainly attributed due to the suppress the recombination of photo excited electrons and hole [45,46].

The photoelectrochemical and electrochemical measurements of prepared materials were performed to study the charge transport property. Fig. 9 (a) shows the transient photocurrent (i-t) response of electrodes prepared by the drop casting of pure ZnO and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite on FTO substrate. The photocurrent response of ZnO/C-dots is 12 times higher than that of pure ZnO, which indicates that suppress in photoexicited electrons and holes recombination rates could be caused by the formation of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure. Further, the enhanced charge separation of the prepared samples also could be confirmed by the EIS measurement. The charge transfer rate at the interface between working electrode and electrolyte solution could be explained radius of the arc on the EIS Nyquist, the smaller radius indicates higher charge transfer rates [47]. Fig. 9 (b) shows EIS Nyquist plot the prepared samples under dark condition, the ZnO/C-dots exhibits the small arc radius when compared with that of pure ZnO, which reviled that the formation of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure is beneficial to improve the charge transfer rate [48].

Based on aforementioned experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism for ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites has been proposed in Fig. 10 . In the photocatalytic mechanism, when the irradiation of UV–Visible light on ZnO, the electrons is excited from VB to CB and leaving the holes in VB. Generally, the photoinduced electrons and holes have tendency to recombine as soon as possible and only limited electrons and holes can reach the electrolyte solution for photocatalytic reaction. Although, in the present case C-dots providing the way to transfer the photoinduced electrons to the surface of photocatalyst, consequently, react with the oxygen in the solution to form oxygen radical (O2 ˙−), which can oxidize the MB dye in solution for degradation [24,49].

Usually, the dye industries produced the wastewater at different pH ranges. Meanwhile, the pH of the aqueous dye solution is an important study for photodegradation processes. Fig. 11 shows the MB dye degradation efficiency on ZnO/c-dots nanocomposites at different pH levels of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11, which was adjusted using diluted HCl and NaOH solutions. The concentration of dye (10 mg/L), catalyst loading (25 mg/L) pH remained constant, and the different pH, ranging from 1 to 11 for presented in the photocatalytic experiments. As shown in Fig. 11, the degradation efficiency of MB corresponds to 60.8%, 64.2%, 69.7%, 75.30%, 86.38% and 94.21% corresponds to pH of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 and 12, respectively. The pH 12 exhibited the maximum photocatalytic dye degradation efficiency ~94.21% was obtained. The photocatalyst has negatively charged through the adsorbing (OH−) ions, whereas the formation of hydroxyl radicals in the photolysis reaction medium [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Further the photocatalytic stability of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite was examined for degradation of MB dye during 5 consecutive cycle experiments (Fig. S1(a)), indicating that degradation efficiency was not significantly declaimed for each cycle. Moreover, the crystalline stability of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite after five cycles of MB degradation was investigated by powder XRD measurements (Fig. S1(b)). The XRD patterns of as prepared ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite and after 5th cycle of photocatalytic degradation experiment shows similar pattern, which implies that, the high stability of prepared materials.

The ZnO hollow sphere and ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites were successfully prepared via solvothermal method. Prepared samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction, FE-SEM, TEM, FTIR, UV–Vis and PL spectroscopic techniques to study the phase formation physiochemical properties. Moreover, the photocatalytic activities of the prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of MB dye under solar light irradiation. Among the prepared photocatalyst, ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites showed a superior photocatalytic activity with degraded efficiency ~96% of MB for within 30 min, respectively. The enhanced phtocatalytic activity of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites is mainly attributed to suppress the charge photoexcited electrons –holes recombination rates and extended light absorption properties. It is expected that these novel, environmentally friendly, high surface area and more photoactive for ZnO/C-dots nanocomposites may promote practical applications of photocatalysts (Five cycles).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
